section.p: Credits directly reduce the amount of tax you owe, dollar for dollar. Certain credits can increase your refund if their combined amount is larger than the tax you owe.
This estimator only supports some credits. There may be other credits you qualify for that could impact your estimated refund or tax owed.
The CTC helps families raising children by providing a tax benefit of up to per qualifying child.

fg-set.question: Number of children
fg-set.hint: Don’t count children without a Social Security number (SSN) valid for work. Leave blank if youboth you and your spouse don’t have an SSN.

section.p: Taxpayers with dependents who don’t qualify for the Child Tax Credit may be able to claim the ODC.

fg-set.question: Number of other dependents
fg-set.hint: Leave blank if you’re not a U.S. citizen, national, or lawful resident. Don't include dependents that you already reported for the Child Tax Credit.

section.p: The CDCC helps taxpayers who pay for the care of a family or household member while working or looking for work.

p.modal-link: Who can claim the CDCC and what expenses are eligible?

fg-set.question: Number of qualifying children and dependents
Estimated annual care expenses for
fg-set.hint: max for one person, and for two or more people

section.p: The EITC helps low- to moderate-income workers and families lower their taxes.

fg-set.question: Number of qualifying children
fg-set.hint: Don't count children without a Social Security number (SSN). Leave blank if you or your spouse don't have an SSN.

fg-set.question: Will you be 25 years old or older on January 1, ?
fg-set.hint: Select "No" if you don't have a Social Security number.

fg-set.question: Will your spouse be 25 years old or older on January 1, ?
fg-set.hint: Select "No" if your spouse doesn't have a Social Security number.

div.p: The retirement savings contribution credit is a tax benefit that helps low- to moderate-income taxpayers save for retirement. Enter the credit amount you and your spouse expect to claim for contributions to a traditional IRA, Roth IRA, 401(k), 403(b), or similar plans.

p.modal-link: Who can claim the retirement savings contribution credit and how do I estimate it?

fg-set.question: Retirement savings credit you and your spouse expect to claim in

section.p: The AOTC helps offset the cost of tuition, fees, and other expenses for the first four years of college.

p.modal-link: Who can claim the AOTC and what expenses qualify?

fg-set.question: Estimated total college tuition or fees in

section.p: The LLC helps offset the cost of college courses, professional certifications, and job skill trainings. You can’t claim the AOTC and LLC for the same student.

fg-set.question: Estimated total tuition or fees in

section.p: The adoption credit helps families offset the costs of adopting an eligible child.

p.modal-link: Who can claim the adoption credit?

fg-set.question: Adoption expenses you and your spouse expect to pay in

section.h3: Credit for the Elderly or the Disabled
section.p: This credit helps older adults and people with qualifying disabilities lower their taxes.

p.modal-link: Who can claim the credit for the elderly or the disabled?

fg-set.question: Amount of the elderly and disabled credit you and your spouse expect to claim in

section.p: The foreign tax credit reduces your U.S. taxes when you pay or owe taxes to a foreign country or U.S. territory.

p.modal-link: Who can claim the foreign tax credit?

fg-set.question: Foreign tax you and your spouse expect to pay in

section.p: Business credits include many individual tax credits that reward businesses for things like hiring and supporting workers, energy investments, or research and development.

fg-set.question: Amount in business credits you and your spouse expect to claim in

section.p: The mortgage interest credit reduces taxes for low- to moderate-income homeowners who receive a Mortgage Credit Certificate from their state or local government.

p.modal-link: Who can claim the mortgage interest credit?

fg-set.question: Estimated mortgage interest credit you and your spouse expect to claim in

section.p: The AMT Credit allows high-income taxpayers to reclaim extra tax paid under the AMT in previous years.

p.modal-link: Who can claim the AMT Credit?

fg-set.question: AMT tax credit you and your spouse expect to claim in

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the Child Tax Credit (CTC)?

modal-content.p: To claim the CTC, you must have a qualifying child who:

ul.li: Is your dependent child, stepchild, foster child, sibling, or descendant (grandchild, niece, nephew)
Is under 17 at the end of
Lives with you in the U.S. more than half the year
Has a Social Security number (SSN) valid for work (you or your spouse must also have an SSN)
Doesn’t support themselves or plans to file a joint return (unless to claim a refund)

modal-content.p: You can claim up to per qualifying child.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 4 on Schedule 8812 for a number greater than 0. Schedule 8812 would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the Credit for Other Dependents (ODC)?

modal-content.p: To claim the ODC, you must have a qualifying dependent who:

ul.li: You plan to claim as a dependent on your tax return
Has a gross income less than
Lives with you in the U.S. more than half the year if they’re a child or for all year if they’re an adult, unless they're a specific type of relative
Has a Social Security number, Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN), or Adoption Tax Identification Number (ATIN)
Doesn’t support themselves or plans to file a joint return
Won’t be used to claim the Child Tax Credit

modal-content.p: You can claim up to per qualifying dependent of any age.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 6 on Schedule 8812 for a number greater than 0. Schedule 8812 would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the Child and Dependent Care Credit (CDCC) and what expenses are eligible?

modal-content.p: To claim the CDCC, you must be paying for the care of a child under 13 or for a spouse or dependent who lives with you and can’t care for themselves.
The person providing the care can’t be your spouse, the child’s parent, or another dependent.
Expenses that qualify:

ul.li: Daycare, preschool (below kindergarten)
Before- and after-school care
Day camps
Care for a spouse or dependent who can’t care for themselves

modal-content.p: Expenses that don’t qualify:

ul.li: Overnight camps
Private school tuition
Food, lodging, or clothing unless directly tied to care
Payments to your spouse, the child’s parent, or another dependent for care

modal-content.p: This is not a complete list. Refer to Publication 503 to learn more about which expenses qualify.
You can claim up to for one person or for two or more people.
If you’re married and filing separately from your spouse, you can only claim this credit if you and your spouse are legally separated or live apart and meet other criteria.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 2 on Schedule 3, which would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)?

modal-content.p: To claim the EITC with children, you must be a low- to moderate-income worker with a qualifying child.
A qualifying child for the EITC must:

ul.li: Be your child, stepchild, foster child, sibling, or descendant (grandchild, niece, nephew)
Be under 19 at the end of , under 24 at the end of if a full-time student, or any age if they are permanently disabled
Live with you in the U.S. more than half the year
Have a Social Security number valid for work
Not file a joint return (except to claim a refund)

modal-content.p: You can claim up to 3 qualifying children.
If you’re married and filing separately from your spouse, you can only claim this credit if you meet specific criteria.
To claim the EITC without children, you must be a low- to moderate-income worker who:

ul.li: Will be at least 25 years old but under 65 on January 1,
Lived in the U.S. for more than half the year
Have a Social Security number valid for work

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 27a on Form 1040 of your federal tax return.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the Retirement Savings Contribution Credit and how do I estimate it?

modal-content.p: To claim the retirement savings contribution credit, you must contribute to a traditional IRA, Roth IRA, 401(k), 403(b), or similar plan.
These contributions don’t qualify:

ul.li: Employer contributions or matches
Rollover contributions

modal-content.p: To estimate your credit amount, multiply the retirement contributions you expect to make in by %. This percentage is based on your filing status and estimated adjusted gross income.
You can claim up to ( if filing jointly).
If it’s hard to estimate, it’s ok to leave this credit amount blank.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: If you expect to have similar retirement savings as last year, you can use your credit amount from last year. Review line 4 on Schedule 3, which would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

p.a: Learn more about the retirement savings contribution credit.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC) and what expenses qualify?

modal-content.p: To claim the AOTC, you, your spouse, or a dependent must:

ul.li: Be in the first 4 years of higher education
Enroll at least half-time to pursue a degree or credential

modal-content.p: You can claim up to per student, including:

ul.li: 100% of the first $2,000 in expenses
25% of the next $2,000 in expenses

modal-content.p: Expenses can include tuition, fees, books, supplies, and other items. You can claim expenses paid by you, your spouse, your dependent, or a third party.
This credit can’t be claimed more than 4 times per student.
You can’t claim both the American Opportunity Tax Credit and the Lifetime Learning Credit for the same student.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 27 on Form 8863, which would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC)?

modal-content.p: To claim the LLC, you, your spouse, or a dependent must:

ul.li: Pay tuition or fees for college courses, professional certifications, or job skill trainings
Enroll or take courses at an eligible educational institution
Enroll in one or more courses for an academic period in

modal-content.p: You can claim up to 20% of the first $10,000 in expenses and a maximum of per federal tax return. You may claim expenses paid by you, your spouse, your dependent, or a third party.
You can’t claim both the American Opportunity Tax Credit and the LLC for the same student.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 31 on Form 8863, which would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the Adoption Credit?

modal-content.p: To claim the adoption credit, you or your spouse must:

ul.li: Have paid qualified adoption expenses such as adoption fees, court costs, attorney fees, or travel
Adopt a child under 18, or who is physically or mentally unable to care for themselves

modal-content.p: You can claim up to in qualified expenses per adopted child. When to claim expenses depends on adoption type, adoption status, and when expenses were incurred.
This credit is partially refundable. It can reduce your taxes and give you a refund up to if it brings your tax owed to $0. Unused amounts can be carried forward for up to 5 years.
If you’re married and filing separately from your spouse, you can only claim this credit if you lived apart from your spouse and meet other criteria.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review Form 8839 (Qualified Adoption Expenses), which would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

p.a: Learn more about the adoption credit.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the elderly or disabled credit?

modal-content.p: You may qualify if:

ul.li: You are 65 years old or older on January 1, , or you retire on permanent and total disability and receive taxable disability income
Your adjusted gross income (AGI) is generally less than $17,500 ($25,000 if married filing jointly)
Your total nontaxable Social Security, pensions, annuities, or disability income is generally less than $5,000 ($7,500 if married filing jointly)

modal-content.p: Use Schedule R to calculate the credit amount.
If you're filing as Married Filing Separately, you can only claim this credit if you meet specific criteria.
If you're filing as Head of Household, you can only claim this credit if you meet specific criteria.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 6d on Schedule 3, which would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

p.a: Learn more about the Elderly or Disabled Credit.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the foreign tax credit?

modal-content.p: To claim the foreign tax credit, you or your spouse must:

ul.li: Pay or owe taxes to a foreign country or U.S. territory, and
Be subject to U.S. tax on the same income

modal-content.p: If you're planning to take an itemized deduction for foreign tax, then you won't be able to claim this credit.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 1 on Schedule 3, which would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

p.a: Learn more about the Foreign Tax Credit.

modal-content.p: Business credits are a group of tax credits that include incentives for activities such as:

ul.li: Hiring and supporting workers
Energy investments
Research and development

modal-content.p: You may need to file Form 3800 to claim them.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 6a on Schedule 3, which would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

p.a: Learn more about business tax credits.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the mortgage interest credit?

modal-content.p: To claim the mortgage interest credit, you and your spouse must:

ul.li: Have a qualified Mortgage Credit Certificate (MCC) from your state or local government
Pay mortgage interest on your main home

modal-content.p: The MCC will show the percentage of interest you can claim as a credit. If you don’t have an MCC, you can’t claim this credit.
This credit is limited to $2,000 per year.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 6g on Schedule 3, which would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.

p.a: Learn more about the Mortgage Interest Credit.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: Who can claim the Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT) Credit?

modal-content.p: To claim the AMT, you or your spouse must:

ul.li: Have owed AMT in a prior year
Not be subject to AMT in the current year

modal-content.p: You can claim this credit by filing Form 8801.

p.strong: How to check if you claimed this credit last year

modal-content.p: Review line 6b on Schedule 3, which would have been attached to and submitted with your Form 1040 federal tax return.
